perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall be held in strict confidence by Receiving Party and shall not be disclosed without prior written consent of Disclosing Party, except to those advisors, affiliates, agents, assigns, attorneys, employees, d irectors, officers and/or members (“Agents”) with a need -to-know the Confidential Information for the purposes of analyzing, implementing or completing the Transactions . Receiving Party shall require all recipients of the Confidential Information to be bo und by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall be held with reasonable care by Receiving Party and shall not be disclosed without prior written consent of Disclosing Party, except to those advisors, affiliates, agents, assigns, attorneys, employees, d irectors, officers and/or members (“Agents”) deemed appropriate by Receiving Party who are informed of the confidential nature of the information. Receiving Party will encourage recipients of Confidential Information to respect its confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'strict confidence' to 'reasonable care' and replacing the 'need-to-know' requirement with 'deemed appropriate' significantly loosens the standard of care. This creates an in-text contradiction within Section 2, as the original text mandates that the 'Receiving Party shall require all recipients of the Confidential Information to be bound by the terms of this Agreement,' while the new text is changed to 'Receiving Party will encourage recipients of Confidential Information to respect its confidentiality'. This weakens the binding nature of the confidentiality, and also goes against the obligations detailed in Section 4, Remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies. The Receiving Party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement. Such remedy shall not be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity. Notwithstanding anything contained in this Agreement, the Receiving Party’s liability to the Disclosing Party in connection with this Agreement and any activities undertaken in connection with the evaluation of the Transactions shall be limite d to direct damages and shall exclude any other liability, including without limitation liability for special, indirect, punitive or consequential damages in contract, tort, warranty, strict liability or otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the confidentiality obligations by changing 'strict confidence' to 'reasonable care' and replacing the 'need-to-know' requirement with 'deemed appropriate'. This directly contradicts the 'Remedies' section, which entitles the Disclosing Party to injunctive or other equitable relief for any breach. The weakened obligation makes it unclear what constitutes a breach, creating a strong contradiction and potential unenforceability issues. The original text mandates recipients be 'bound by the terms', where the change encourages recipients only 'to respect its confidentiality', weakening obligations.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Receiving Party is requested or required by legal or regulatory a uthority to disclose any Confidential Information, the Receiving Party shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party of such request or requirement prior to disclosure, if permitted by law, so that Disclosing Party may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Receiving Party is requested or required by legal or regulatory authority to disclose any Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will use commercially reasonable efforts to notify the Disclosing Party of such request or requirement prior to disclosure, if practical, so that Disclosing Party may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly notify' to 'will use commercially reasonable efforts to notify' and adding 'if practical' introduces ambiguity. The original text placed a clear obligation on the Receiving Party to notify the Disclosing Party, whereas the modified text allows for interpretation of what constitutes 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'practical'. This alteration introduces an internal contradiction if other sections assume immediate notification, and contradicts Section 2 where it is expected that information is treated with 'reasonable care'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nondisclosure and Use of Confidential Information. Confidential Information shall be held with reasonable care by Receiving Party and shall not be disclosed without prior written consent of Disclosing Party, except to those advisors, affiliates, agents, assigns, attorneys, employees, d irectors, officers and/or members (“Agents”) deemed appropriate by Receiving Party who are informed of the confidential nature of the information. Receiving Party will encourage recipients of Confidential Information to respect its confidentiality. Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Agents. The Receiving Party shall use the same degree of care to protect the Confidential Information as the Receiving Party employs to protect its own information of like importance, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care based on industry standard.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated prompt notification to the Disclosing Party in case of a request for confidential information disclosure. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and adds 'if practical,' introducing ambiguity. This directly contradicts the 'reasonable care' standard outlined in the contradicted text regarding the handling of confidential information. The 'reasonable care' requirement implies a consistent and reliable standard, whereas the modified text allows for subjective interpretation and potentially less stringent notification efforts. This creates an in-text contradiction by potentially allowing the Receiving Party to take actions (or inactions) regarding notification that would be deemed non-compliant with the 'reasonable care' standard.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement. Such remedy shall not be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any material breach of this Agreement and that Disclosing Party may be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any material breach of this Agreement. Such remedy shall be considered but may not be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'material' before 'breach' and changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may be entitled,' and further modifying 'shall not be the exclusive remedy' to 'shall be considered but may not be the exclusive remedy', this change weakens the Disclosing Party's remedies. This change creates a contradiction within Section 4 itself. The original text provided a definitive right to injunctive relief, whereas the modified version makes it discretionary. Moreover, this contradicts the clause that states 'Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement' in section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nondisclosure and Use of Confidential Information. Confidential Information shall be held with reasonable care by Receiving Party and shall not be disclosed without prior written consent of Disclosing Party, except to those advisors, affiliates, agents, assigns, attorneys, employees, d irectors, officers and/or members (“Agents”) deemed appropriate by Receiving Party who are informed of the confidential nature of the information. Receiving Party will encourage recipients of Confidential Information to respect its confidentiality. Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Agents. The Receiving Party shall use the same degree of care to protect the Confidential Information as the Receiving Party employs to protect its own information of like importance, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care based on industry standard.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the Disclosing Party's remedies by adding 'material' before 'breach', changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may be entitled,' and modifying 'shall not be the exclusive remedy' to 'shall be considered but may not be the exclusive remedy'. The original text provided a definitive right to injunctive relief, whereas the modified version makes it discretionary. This change contradicts the clause in the Contradicted Text that states 'Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Agents' because by adding 'material' breach, any non-material breach is not covered, creating a contradiction in obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly fol lowing any decision by the Receiving Party not to continue discussions with respect to the Transactions , and at any other time upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy, at the Receiving Party’s option, all wr itten Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, including that portion of such Confidential Information that may be found in analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by, or for, the Receiving Party, and the Receiving Party and it s Agents shall not retain any copies of such written Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Following any decision by the Receiving Party not to continue discussions with respect to the Transactions, and at any other time upon the Disclosing Party’s reasonable written request, the Receiving Party shall use reasonable efforts to return or destroy, at the Receiving Party’s discretion, all written Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to the extent reasonably practicable, including that portion of such Confidential Information that may be found in analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by, or for, the Receiving Party, and the Receiving Party and its Agents shall make reasonable efforts not to retain any copies of such written Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Promptly' and adding 'reasonable' throughout the sentence, the obligation to return or destroy confidential information has been significantly weakened. This introduction of 'reasonable efforts' and 'to the extent reasonably practicable' creates an in-text contradiction as it conflicts with the strict requirements established initially for handling confidential information, especially regarding its potential disclosure as described in sections 1 and 2. This undermines Section 1 and 2, by allowing for the 'reasonable' retention of the information when the standard of protection is 'strict'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information. “Confidential Information” shall mean all information, regardless of the form in which it is communicated or maintained (whether oral, written, electronic or vi sual) and whether prepared by Company or otherwise, which is disclosed to Counterp arty, regardless of whether such information is disclosed intentionally or inadvertently, before or after the execution of this Agreement, in connection with the Transaction and including all records, reports, analyses, notes, memoranda, documentation, knowled ge, data, specifications, diagrams, statistics, systems or software, manuals, business plans, operational information or practices, processes (whether or not patented, patentable or reduced to practice), customer lists, concepts, ideas, policies, contractu al arrangements with, and information about, the Company ’s suppliers, distributors and customers, the existence of the discussions between the Parties concerning the Transaction, or other information that are based on, contain or reflect any such Confident ial Information. All information received from the Company shall be considered Confidential Information, unless it is specifically designated as non -proprietary and non - confidential. Confidential Information shall not include: (a) information which is o r becomes publicly available regardless of whether it results from a violation of this Agreement; (b) information which is or becomes available on a non - confidential basis from any source; (c) information which the Receiving Party can demonstrate was in its possession prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or (d) information which is developed by or for Receiving Party independently of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information by adding 'reasonable efforts' and 'to the extent reasonably practicable.' This contradicts the original text's stricter requirement to 'return or destroy' at the Receiving Party's option, which is further compounded by the reasonable effort to not retain the information when the implicit standard was to not retain the information at all. This creates a strong contradiction with the contradicted text, which broadly defines 'Confidential Information' and implies a strict standard of protection, regardless of how it was disclosed.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall terminate three (3 ) year s following written notice by either Party to the other of its desire to terminate th is Agreement. However, the obligations contained herein shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date the Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate three (3 ) year s following written notice by either Party to the other of its desire to terminate th is Agreement, unless otherwise agreed in writing. However, the obligations contained herein shall remain in effect for a period that may be extended beyond five (5) years from the date the Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement, subject to ongoing negotiations between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'unless otherwise agreed in writing' and 'that may be extended beyond five (5) years' this creates ambiguity around the termination date and the duration of confidentiality obligations. The original text provided a clear five-year period, while the modified text allows for extensions via 'ongoing negotiations'. This introduces an in-text contradiction with the defined term and timeline originally set in the agreement, creating potential enforceability issues since the term is no longer clearly defined. This directly contradicts Section 1, as the 'Confidential Information' could indefinitely be considered so.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information. “Confidential Information” shall mean all information, regardless of the form in which it is communicated or maintained (whether oral, written, electronic or vi sual) and whether prepared by Company or otherwise, which is disclosed to Counterp arty, regardless of whether such information is disclosed intentionally or inadvertently, before or after the execution of this Agreement, in connection with the Transaction and including all records, reports, analyses, notes, memoranda, documentation, knowled ge, data, specifications, diagrams, statistics, systems or software, manuals, business plans, operational information or practices, processes (whether or not patented, patentable or reduced to practice), customer lists, concepts, ideas, policies, contractu al arrangements with, and information about, the Company ’s suppliers, distributors and customers, the existence of the discussions between the Parties concerning the Transaction, or other information that are based on, contain or reflect any such Confident ial Information. All information received from the Company shall be considered Confidential Information, unless it is specifically designated as non -proprietary and non - confidential. Confidential Information shall not include: (a) information which is o r becomes publicly available regardless of whether it results from a violation of this Agreement; (b) information which is or becomes available on a non - confidential basis from any source; (c) information which the Receiving Party can demonstrate was in its possession prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or (d) information which is developed by or for Receiving Party independently of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the termination clause and confidentiality obligations, introducing ambiguity by stating that the agreement may terminate 'unless otherwise agreed in writing' and that confidentiality obligations 'may be extended beyond five (5) years', which contradicts the original intention of a fixed term. This directly contradicts the 'Confidential Information' section, as the 'Confidential Information' could indefinitely be considered so, when there are exceptions to what it considers confidential information (information that becomes public, etc.). The original text defined what information would be considered confidential, and under what context, where this text introduces ambiguity of when this information would no longer be considered confidential, directly creating conflict.
